title: Fire Involving Sealed Sources
narrationScript: A sealed source is built to survive a fire — its shielding is not. Lead melts at six hundred twenty-one degrees Fahrenheit, and a fully developed fire runs far hotter, so a device can come out of the flames with its capsule intact and its shielding gone, looking completely normal. A long, fuel-fed fire can even breach the capsule and turn an exposure problem into contamination riding the smoke. Your response follows one ranking: people, then the source, then property. Warn and evacuate, retract the source only if it is quick and safe, call nine-one-one yourself and say radioactive material is involved, then notify the RSO and hold a perimeter upwind. When the incident commander arrives, you are the radiological expert on scene: give the nuclide, the activity, the exact location, the shielding status, your survey readings, and the protection crews need. After the fire, nobody re-enters until a survey proves the area safe — and the RSO makes the required regulatory reports.

sections.heading: Fire Changes the Problem
sections.body:
A radiographic **sealed source** is built like nothing else on the job site. The radioactive material — iridium-192, cobalt-60, or selenium-75 — sits inside a welded stainless-steel capsule, typically doubly encapsulated and certified as **special form** radioactive material: tested to survive impact, percussion, bending, and a furnace at **800 °C (1472 °F) for 10 minutes** without releasing its contents. The exposure device itself is commonly certified as a **Type B transport package**, engineered against a fully engulfing 800 °C fire lasting 30 minutes. In most fires, the capsule comes through intact.

What fire attacks is everything *around* the capsule:

- **Lead melts at 327 °C (621 °F).** A fully developed structure or vehicle fire runs roughly 800–1000 °C, so lead shielding in storage containers and older equipment slumps away early — leaving a source poorly shielded inside a housing that can still look normal from the outside.
- **Depleted uranium**, the shield inside most gamma cameras, does not melt until 1132 °C — but in a long, hot fire it **oxidizes**, and uranium-oxide particles ride the smoke: a low-level radioactive and chemically toxic contaminant.
- **Locks, drive cables, and guide tubes** burn or seize, so control of the source position can be lost.
- In a prolonged, fuel-fed fire that exceeds the certification tests, even the **capsule can breach** — and a sealed-source job becomes loose contamination spread by smoke, ash, and fire-fighting water.

That is the threat model in one sentence: fire rarely destroys the source, but it can strip the shielding and, in the worst case, open the capsule. Your response assumes both until a survey proves otherwise.

sections.caption: Temperature ladder for a sealed-source fire: lead shielding fails at 327 °C — far below a fully developed fire — while the special-form capsule is engineered to ride out 800 °C.

sections.heading: First Moves: People, the Crank, the Call
sections.body:
The written operating and emergency procedures required by **10 CFR 34.45** spell out your duties, and they follow one ranking — **people, then the source, then property**:

1. **Warn and evacuate.** Raise the alarm, get everyone moving away from both the fire and the radiation area, and account for the whole crew at a safe assembly point. A missing person changes everything the fire department does next.
2. **Secure the source — only if you safely can.** If the fire is small and just starting and your path to the crank is clear, retract the source to its shielded position and lock it. Those few seconds remove the radiation field for every firefighter who follows. If the fire is developed, spreading, or between you and the crank — **leave it**. Nobody approaches an unshielded gamma source to save equipment.
3. **Call 911 — immediately and yourself.** Do not assume someone else called. Tell the dispatcher plainly: *radioactive material is involved — a sealed industrial radiography source*. That single sentence puts the radiological machinery in motion before the first engine rolls.
4. **Notify the RSO** through the licensee's emergency call list (covered fully in lesson rs-6-4), and set a perimeter **upwind** while you wait — with your survey meter if it survived, by pacing off distance if it did not.

Then stay. Until the RSO arrives, you are the only person on scene who knows what is burning.

sections.title: No source is worth a life — or a re-entry
sections.body: Never enter or re-enter a burning building or vehicle to rescue an exposure device — not for the schedule, not for the license, not for the cost of the source. Turnout gear and SCBA protect firefighters against **contamination**, not against the **gamma field** of an unshielded source — and they protect you against neither. The source can be recovered later under controlled, metered conditions. A dose taken in smoke and panic cannot be returned.

sections.heading: Holding the Scene While It Burns
sections.body:
Until the fire department takes command, scene control is yours; after they arrive, you advise. The working rules:

- **Stage upwind and uphill.** Once shielding or capsule damage is possible, smoke is treated as potentially radioactive. Move the assembly point if the wind shifts.
- **Use the ERG numbers as a starting floor.** The DOT *Emergency Response Guidebook* (Guide 163, radioactive materials) calls for isolating **at least 25 meters (75 feet) in all directions**, expanding to an initial evacuation of about **300 meters (1000 feet)** when a large quantity is involved in a major fire. Your survey meter — not the guidebook, and not your eye — sets the final boundary.
- **Fire-fighting technique does not change.** ERG guidance is explicit: the presence of radioactive material should not influence fire-control methods. Crews fight the fire normally — wearing **SCBA**, approaching upwind, keeping **time** near the device short and **distance** long. Undamaged devices and containers may be moved out of the fire zone when it can be done without risk; damaged ones are left where they lie.
- **Track who goes in.** Note which responders work near the device location and for how long — that log is the raw material for any dose assessment later.
- **Nobody handles the device**, and anything removed from the area — tools, fittings, debris — stays inside the line until it is surveyed.

sections.heading: Briefing the Responders: Say It So They Can Use It
sections.body:
When the incident commander steps off the engine, you deliver the radiological picture in under a minute — specific, numeric, and calm:

- **What:** a sealed industrial radiography source — a gamma emitter; an external radiation hazard, with contamination possible if the shielding or capsule burns.
- **Which, and how much:** the radionuclide and activity. *Iridium-192, 84 curies* tells a hazmat officer more than a paragraph of adjectives.
- **Where, exactly:** building, bay, and position — *northeast corner of the fab shop, ten feet inside the roll-up door, in the steel storage box*.
- **Shielded — or not:** whether the source was locked in its shielded position or exposed in the guide tube when the fire started. This is the single fact that most changes their tactics.
- **What you measured:** your last survey readings and where your boundary stands.
- **What to do about it:** SCBA, upwind approach, maximize distance, minimize time near the device location, touch nothing near it, and survey anything that leaves the area.
- **Who to call:** the RSO's name and 24-hour number — and, for a vehicle fire, the shipping papers and emergency-response information carried in the cab.

Then stay with command. The questions will keep coming, and the answers must come from you until the RSO arrives.

sections.caption: The responder briefing, item by item — what to say and why it matters.
rows: Radioactive material involved
“Sealed radiography source — gamma emitter”
Triggers radiological protocols and hazmat support (ERG Guide 163)
Radionuclide and activity
“Iridium-192, 84 curies”
Scales the hazard and the protective distances
Shielding status
“Cranked in and locked” — or “exposed in the guide tube”
An unshielded source adds an extreme local gamma field to the fire
Exact location
“NE corner, 10 ft inside the roll-up door”
Lets crews place attack lines while keeping time near it short
Survey readings and boundary
“2 mR/h at my cones, 60 ft out”
Confirms where staging and work are safe
Protective measures
“SCBA, upwind, distance, no handling”
Turnout gear stops contamination — not gamma
RSO contact / shipping papers
24-hour phone number; papers from the vehicle cab
Brings licensee authority and recovery expertise into play

sections.caption: Communication flow for a fire involving a sealed source: 911 first, the RSO without delay, the incident commander briefed on arrival — and the RSO carries the 10 CFR 30.50 telephone reports to the regulator.

sections.heading: After the Fire: Survey First, Then Report
sections.body:
The flames are out, the building is steaming, and overhaul crews want in. Hold the line — **nobody re-enters the area around the device until a radiation survey is made**:

- **Approach meter-first.** A qualified person — you, under the RSO's direction — walks in watching the trend, ready to back out. Assume the shielding is compromised until the readings prove otherwise: a camera can sit there looking intact with its lead melted or its depleted uranium oxidized inside.
- **Treat ash, debris, and runoff near the device as contaminated** until wipe tests say otherwise. If the capsule breached, contamination travels on boots, gloves, and water.
- **The device is done working.** It stays secured under continuous control as licensed material, gets leak-tested by qualified personnel, and goes nowhere except into a recovery and disposal path run by people licensed to do it. If the source cannot be found in the debris, it is *lost licensed material* — with an immediate telephone-report duty of its own under 10 CFR 20.2201.
- **The RSO makes the regulatory calls.** Under **10 CFR 30.50**, an event that prevents immediate protective actions — fire is the listed example — requires a telephone report to the NRC Operations Center **within 4 hours**; an unplanned fire that damages licensed material or the device containing it requires one **within 24 hours** (a radiography source exceeds the quantity threshold by orders of magnitude). A written report follows within 30 days, and Agreement States impose compatible requirements. Your personal part of that chain — who you call, when, and what you say — is the whole subject of lesson rs-6-4.

figures.title: What fire attacks: melting points vs. real fire temperatures

interactive.title: Shop Fire: Make Every Call Right
interactive.intro: Mid-morning in a fabrication shop. Your 84-curie iridium-192 source is at the end of the guide tube, mid-exposure, when a trash fire flares up twenty feet from your setup. Walk the emergency from first flame to overhaul — every step has one defensible answer.

steps.prompt: The fire is small and just starting, and your path to the crank stays clear. First move?
options: Grab an extinguisher and attack the fire before it grows
Crank the source back to its shielded position and lock it, then raise the alarm
Pull the guide tube and exposed source away from the flames by hand
Leave immediately and let the fire department handle everything
steps.feedback: Seconds at the crank remove the gamma field for everyone who responds after you — and your path is safe. Handling the guide tube with the source out invites a massive dose, fighting the fire first leaves the field up behind you, and walking away without warning anyone abandons both the crew and an exposed source.

steps.prompt: The fire reaches stacked pallets and cable insulation — beyond any extinguisher now — and flames stand between you and the device. The source is shielded and locked. Next?
options: Evacuate and account for the crew, call 911 yourself, and tell the dispatcher radioactive material — a sealed radiography source — is involved
Push past the flames to carry the camera out, since it is shielded and safe to handle
Call the RSO first and wait for instructions before calling the fire department
Back the truck up to the door so the crew can load equipment out
steps.feedback: People clear, head count taken, 911 called with the radiological warning attached — that one sentence mobilizes hazmat before the first engine rolls. The RSO call comes without delay, but never ahead of life safety and the fire department, and nobody walks through flame for replaceable equipment.

steps.prompt: The incident commander steps off the engine and asks, 'What have you got?' Your briefing:
options: 'There's a radiography camera in there — better keep everyone way back and just let it burn out.'
'I can't discuss the source — that information is restricted to licensee personnel.'
'The source is fireproof, so there is no radiological concern at all.'

'Iridium-192 sealed source, 84 curies, shielded and locked, in the steel daybox ten feet inside the northeast roll-up door. Fight the fire normally — SCBA, approach upwind, keep time near that corner short. Nobody handles the box, and my RSO is on the phone now.'
steps.feedback: Nuclide, activity, location, shielding status, tactics, and contacts — in one breath. ERG Guide 163 is explicit that radioactive material does not change fire-fighting technique, none of this information is restricted, and the capsule's toughness never erases the shielding question.

steps.prompt: The fire is out. Overhaul crews are ready to pull ceiling and shovel debris — right through the corner where the daybox sat. The IC asks if it is safe.
options: Yes — the source was shielded when the fire started, so nothing has changed
Yes, as long as the daybox still looks intact from the doorway
Not yet — no one works that corner until a metered radiation survey is made, and debris and runoff stay inside the line until surveyed
No one may enter for 24 hours — that is the federal rule after any fire involving radioactive material
steps.feedback: Shielding state is invisible from the outside — lead melts at 327 °C and every real fire gets there. The survey, not appearance and not an imaginary waiting period, reopens that corner. There is no 24-hour entry rule; there is an absolute survey-first rule.

checkQuestions.question: A loaded exposure device is caught in a fully developed shop fire (roughly 800–1000 °C). The MOST likely radiological outcome is:
options: The sealed capsule melts immediately while the shielding survives
The capsule survives, but lead shielding components can melt away — leaving a source poorly shielded inside a device that may look normal
The heat sterilizes the source, destroying its radioactivity
Nothing changes, because exposure devices are certified fireproof in any fire
checkQuestions.explanation: Lead melts at 327 °C (621 °F) — far below real fire temperatures — while the welded stainless special-form capsule is tested to 800 °C for 10 minutes and stainless does not melt until about 1400 °C. No temperature destroys radioactivity; only decay reduces it.

checkQuestions.question: Fire breaks out near your setup during an exposure. The flames are small and just starting, and your path to the crank is clear. The best first action is to:
options: Attack the fire with an extinguisher so it cannot spread
Carry the guide tube and exposed source outside, away from the flames
Retract the source to its shielded, locked position — then alert others and address the fire
Evacuate immediately without touching the controls or warning anyone
checkQuestions.explanation: When it can be done quickly and without risk, securing the source eliminates the radiation hazard for everyone who responds afterward. Handling the guide tube with the source exposed invites an extreme dose, and leaving the source out hands the fire department an invisible second hazard.

checkQuestions.question: An engine company arrives at your vehicle fire. Which briefing belongs with the incident commander?
options: Radionuclide and activity, exact location of the device, whether the source is shielded, your latest survey readings, and the protective measures crews should take
The exposure technique, film type, and how many welds remain on the shift
Only the device model and serial number — source details are proprietary
Nothing — radiographers may not discuss source information with non-licensee personnel
checkQuestions.explanation: Responders need the hazard (what and how much), its location and shielding status, what has been measured, and what protection to use — plus the RSO's contact and, for a vehicle, the shipping papers. None of that is restricted, and withholding it endangers the crews.

checkQuestions.question: Per Emergency Response Guidebook guidance for radioactive materials (Guide 163), what should firefighters be told about attacking a fire involving a sealed source?
options: Water must never be applied anywhere near a radioactive source
They must stand by until the source decays to a safe level
Turnout gear and SCBA stop the gamma radiation, so no special positioning is needed
The radioactive material does not change fire-fighting technique — fight it with SCBA, from upwind, keeping time near the device short and distance long
checkQuestions.explanation: ERG Guide 163 states that the presence of radioactive material should not influence fire-control techniques. SCBA and turnout gear protect against contamination — not the external gamma field — so time and distance remain the only protection from a possibly unshielded source.

checkQuestions.question: The fire is out and the structure looks safe to enter. Before overhaul crews work near where the exposure device sat, what must happen?
options: A 24-hour cooling-off period so radiation levels can dissipate
A radiation survey — shielding can be melted or burned away with no visible sign, and only the meter can prove the area safe
A visual check that the device looks intact, which confirms the source is shielded
A washdown of the area to rinse residual radiation away
checkQuestions.explanation: Gamma fields do not dissipate on a schedule — iridium-192 loses half its activity only every 74 days — and appearance proves nothing about internal shielding. Hosing the area can actually spread contamination. A metered survey is the only valid gate for re-entry.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §10.2 (fire involving sealed sources)
10 CFR Part 34, §34.45 — operating and emergency procedures (accident notification and minimizing exposure of persons in an accident)
10 CFR Part 30, §30.50 — reporting requirements: 4-hour and 24-hour telephone reports for fires and events involving licensed material
DOT Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), Guide 163 — Radioactive Materials (Low to High Level Radiation)
